About PhD

Even though I passed my Viva successfully a year ago, I will be having my graduation in a couple of days at the moment of writing this page. Thus, there is no better time than now to reflect and recap the outcomes of my research project. I also hope this would help others decide whether to consider a Marie Skłodowska-Curie Actions (MSCA) Fellowship or dive into the topic I studied.


I realised that there are quite a few to be written about and decided to split them into three posts:

  • A brief summary of the PhD project
  • A checklist for the development of qAOP models
  • Key learnings as a Marie Curie PhD Fellow

Other quick links

  • The PhD thesis, which is open access from the day one after obtaining the PhD title
  • A visual summary of the PhD thesis

Some other thoughts

I was told that it is about being the right person at the right time. Who knows, it might have been so. But I can argue that no matter what, it is also about the person, the spirit, motivation and willingness to spend 100% of the time, and sometimes even more, on the research topic; the resources, e.g., the scholarship itself offers a decent life with the note that it depends on the country where you are mainly located as the prices have escalated everywhere these days; and the teams, exactly multiple teams each with its role. Think of it as a puzzle: science is multidisciplinary.
A couple of months ago, I was asked if everything that my PhD has was developed by me. I initially thought it to be a joke. Then, I took it as a tricky question asked to test me but surprisingly, someone can indeed, find it to have been a lot for someone to carry so much in less than three and a half years (four to six years is the average). Still, I am not the one to judge and will leave it to the others. This reminded me that it is difficult to appreciate your work, and each has a personal perspective on things.

Whatever you choose, follow it wholeheartedly and appreciate the journey itself 🦋
